sentences.text: Yes.
He was buried in a Jewish cemetery close to a fence.
We had no marking.
And one of the older men wrote down his name on a, on a plywood with his name and, and when he passed away.
There's no such think as any, any religious or anything.
If a human being, whoever it was, was a Jew or a Russian or anybody, it was just throw him in in a hole and cover up.
That was the end of it.
There's no religious service at, at all.
Yes.
Yes.
They were bombing.
After that, there was still fighting was going on.
A lot of troops were still in the woods, Russian troops.
And from time to time, we could hear the planes were flying overhead.
And the bombings was until they gave up or, or they killed them.
I don't know what happened.
No.
There were, was--
There were no kids my age.
And mostly, with men, we tried to stick together.
And everybody had feel sorry for me, because I was one of the youngest.
Mostly the men were in the late 30s, 40s approximately.
But I was only 16, 17 years old.
Yes.
No, no.
No.
No.
He did not teach me.
I just was a school boy, nothing else.
I just loaf around, you know, play with, with young people over there.
I got acquainted with some local people-- I mean, Jewish, Jewish families.
When, when my father-- after that, when my father was killed or dead, I was left alone.
And I didn't know what to do.
So I was wandering in the streets.
And then I know some Jewish families over there, which I didn't know the names or anything else.
But I just walked in.
And the soldiers saw me.
They had also children.
They told me, come in already, come in.
And they told me I can stay with them.
And there was one family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had quite a few people already.
We used to call them [NON-ENGLISH] or a displaced person.
And they somehow were a well-to-do family.
And they were-- the, the older people used to work over there in the-- they had big barns with corn and wheat.
That's the family I was living over there.
No.
That was, that, that was a little bit later after that.
Just a regular laborer like everybody else.
That--
Yes.
Definitely.
It was not a ghetto yet.
But we had to show up for work.
We used to get, I think, some, some cards.
We used to buy some bread or some food we used to get for that.
And after a few weeks, we had to show up every day in a certain time.
In, in, in the headquarters from the German Army over there.
It was a little bit on-- it used to be a school over there.
So they took over the school.
And that was the-- I don't know if it was the headquarters or just a quarters for the German troops.
And in 1942, they already transferred us to, to Baranowitsche.
We were put in cars, in the military trucks.
And they brought us over to the station, to the station where they kept the slave labor.
There were camps from prisoners of war, big camps already.
And we were the only civilians.
And we start building, we start building the station.
But in meantime, that also they took us out to dig graves.
It was ditches.
We didn't know what the heck was going to go in over there.
They were already catching some-- either they were soldiers or partisans, used to call it.
We didn't know who they were.
They had no insignia, no military insignia, except they had Russian uniforms.
And almost on a daily basis, they get a few.
They bring them out in a cemetery.
It was a Christian cemetery, a big cemetery.
And they shoot them.
We were staying maybe 15, 20 feet behind.
They told us, you go over there.
And sometimes we maybe you couldn't see it.
So we used to, we used to almost lay down on the floor.
And they shoot them.
We had to complete the job.
Meaning what?
Some of them fall in in the holes.
And some were outside.
We had to throw them in.
And some were not even dead.
And we used to point out that the man is not dead.
One of, one of the officers used to go with a pistol and finish him off, he shouldn't suffer anymore.
They were almost dead.
But he still-- his body still moved.
So they used to give him an extra bullet or shoot him in.
And this was almost on a daily basis.
Later on, it was summer time, they took a big group of people.
We should be prepare big ditches.
So we assumed that this must be some other killings.
And that particular time that day, there were a lot of high-ranking officers.
I don't know the ranks.
But I saw with the insignias, the whole thing, they must be big shots.
And they took a whole group of-- had to be prisoners, no ranks or anything else on their body.
There were at least 70, 80 soldiers and from-- Russian soldiers, or Russian prisoners, or Russian partisan.
They used to say it's partisan.
And they brought them over over there.
On one side was a small hill.
They took off-- everybody had to take off the shoes and the clothing.
They lined them up.
On one side was the prisoners.
And the other side was regular German soldiers with-- there were no machine-- They didn't use machine guns.
They used regular rifles.
And some of them, I could see they were crossing themselves.
Some didn't do nothing.
Because they were so exhausted that they didn't ask.
They were waiting they should, should, should get shot.
And finally, we could hear the, the shot from, from all those rifles.
We were behind over there.
And it was horrible.
So many young men were all killed.
[CRYING] They, they were all were killed.
[DRINKS]
And some fell down in the ditch.
Some were still on top.
We had to push them down.
And we didn't know.
Are we next?
But somehow, luckily, it looks like the human mind works different.
We did our job.
And they brought us back to the camp, to the labor camp.
After that, knowing what's going on and seeing what's happening, I made up my mind.
I'm not going to be one of them.
And they took us back after that big execution.
For, for about a week or 10 days, they didn't take anybody to the cemetery.
Either they stopped shooting for a, for a few days, or they were usually gathering bigger groups.
If they-- if they used to catch in the woods one or two, they used to put him in a prison.
There was a-- they made a prison in, in, in the, in the building.
Because we used to go in the building.
So we know what's going on.
On top of the building used to be machine guns.
And they used to take us.
Since we were all civilian, we didn't know what's happening.
So we used to carry the ammunition, those boxes, way on top, on the top roof.
Because they were expecting some, some attack from partisan or whatever.
There were already groups of partisan or, or ex-soldiers that did, that did not surrender.
And, and at that time, the situation changed.
They were more careful with, with shooting.
Because everybody knew what was happening that particular day.
And we went back to work at the station over there, digging in the sand, filling in those cars.
And at lunchtime, they were already-- pardon me.
I have to go back.
Mostly, those soldiers who were watching us were older Germans.
They were not the young fellows like that were going to the front.
But they're watching us and building that station.
And during lunchtime, they, they give us a little freedom, the civilian, not the military.
And we used to sneak out, go around, look what's going on.
We used to go over there.
Close by were also farmers, small farms, farmhouses.
We used to go and beg for a piece of bread.
Sometimes they, they prepared some bread for us.
Sometimes they told, we don't have any more.
And then I find out that there is-- maybe a couple feet from me was a, a major street going to the airport.
Well, the camp was at one place where they kept us, because we were building the new station.
And not too far was the airport, maybe a half a mile, a mile.
One day, when people were going to the market-- I knew already that there's a certain days-- it was middle of the week-- the farmers used to bring food to the market in exchange for goods or money.
I don't know.
And used to be a lot, a lot of people walking in the street.
At noon, I decided-- oh, also, we had already the yellow stars.
And, And I took off the yellow star.
And I went through a small place where, where a few trees already.
And I saw that people are walking.
I just ran first in between those old people across the highway.
And I took off.
Nobody knew about it, because it was still lunch time.
One was laying in one place, one the other place.
But after lunch, every day, they used to count if you have all the people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that particular day, I went on the highway.
And I saw some cattle over there.
And a young boy was over there.
So I ran over to him.
And I, I told him the whole-- where can we go-- where can I go around?
He showed me with a hand, don't go that way because there is the airport.
And there's Germans.
You go straight down.
And maybe you will be lucky.
He, he didn't know if I am a Jew, or, or, or a Russian soldiers, or a prisoner.
Because I had a, a jacket from one of those dead people, unfortunately.
I put it on, because we had no clothes.
And when we took all the clothes when they were shooting, after that, we used to bring it back to the, to the headquarters, or, or a camp, or school, or whatever it was over there.
So one of them was a-- a [? gentleman ?] told me, why don't you put on one?
And I took one.
Because I was almost half naked.
I had no clothes.
And that particular day-- so he looked at me.
He didn't know if I, if I am Russian or whoever I am.
And he told me, go.
And that day, I took off fast.
And I went over the highway.
And I went in close to the woods.
There's an awful lot of woods.
Wherever you go, there's wooded area over there.
You have a farm, 50 feet away, there's already trees or a small, small woods.
And I took off.
And about half an hour later, I could hear shooting in the air from, from rifles.
It looks like they find out that one is missing, so they had to make a report.
I find out later on, they told them, we caught him who escaped.
And, and we killed him.
That was me.
Because somebody else did the same thing once they find out I escaped.
And I walked the half a day and the night.
Once I went away from the big city, there were lots of farms.
I got in one place.
And I knocked at the door.
Please give me a piece of bread.
And she said, [NON-ENGLISH] Jew?
I don't know.
Either she got scared, or she didn't know who-- what it is.
And I ran out.
I didn't wait what she's going to do.
And I know that in all those small places, there were already local police that worked with the Germans, collaborators.
But I ran fast.
I went again, another street but always tried to go close where I saw trees, a wooded area.
And I got in over there.
And all night, I walked around in the woods, a half a day and at night.
I didn't know where I'm going.
It's just luck.
But I could see in the woods already signs like arrows cut in the trees.
So I assumed there must be some partisan someplace.
But do I go in the right area or not?
I don't know.
And suddenly in the middle of the night came down raining with buckets.
You never saw anything in your life, such heavy rain.
And I got to a farm over there.
And I knocked at the door.
And the farmer told me, come in.
He let me sit at the stove.
That was the heating system.
He tells me, you go ahead, go in in, in, in the stable over there.
Lay down a little bit.
I'll wake you up.
And we were already far away from the city already.
But he told me, you have to take off early in the morning.
Because Germans come over here.
And they cut the trees.
They need the wood.
And after that, I got up in the morning.
I felt really good already.
Because after a, a half a day and a night running and running as, as fast as I could, he directed me to an area and told me, you will meet some friends of yours, partisans.
I didn't know what partisans means even.
I had no guns.
I have nothing.
I had no military training.
Even if somebody would give me a gun, I wouldn't know what to do with it.
But anyway, I went over there.
And on the second day, which is a half a day and night, and the second day, I came to an area with heavy woods.
And I could see the people are singing.
And they already were-- they were not afraid.
And I came over and asked them, where is the, the headquarter?
Where is the partisan?
They showed me, go in.
There's a building over there.
I came in over there, and there were some partisans with guns, the whole thing.
They were, they were, they were already fighting, fighting people.
So they, they saw me.
So one of the officers told me, how old are you?
Where are you from?
You know, starting to find out what I was doing.
So I told them, look, I was working in the camp.
And I was escaped.
I am a Jew.
They looked at me.
Because there were cases where somebody escaped.
And the Russians would kill them themselves or shoot them themselves, because they were afraid.
They looked at me once, twice, and tells me, you go down that highway over here.
It's not a highway, actually.
It's a, it's a small way to go in deep in the woods.
And there is a, a, there is some Jewish camp.
And there were a family camp.
And I got over there.
They were really surprised.
There were, were already patrols, Jewish patrols.
They took me in.
But they started talking to me in Yiddish and explaining what I did and how come.
They took me in.
And I joined that group.
Go ahead.
We speak mostly Polish and Yiddish.
But it is-- the Yiddish was mixed in with German.
Yes.
A little bit better than the Russian.
Yes.
I spoke already almost fluently.
Because young kids pick up any language very fluently.
Because I was in school from '41-- no, no, '40-- 1940 till '41, almost a year and a half, close to two years.
Yes.
Yes.
As soon we got on the transport from that horrible place where we were burying the people, we were required to have Jewish stars.
It approximately would be '42, by the end of-- about '42, approximately, in the wintertime, we already were required to, to, to have stars, Jewish stars.
No, the early 19--
It's well, it's a possibility.
But they spoke to me Yiddish.
The Russian, no-- I spoke fluently Russian.
I explain it to him.
They looked at me.
And I told them what I was doing over there.
I gave my whole explanation.
And they told me, go over there.
Can I have a piece of bread?
He, he shook his head.
He told me, go ahead and eat.
They gave me some soup and bread.
And then he tells me, go over there, straight ahead.
And you will join the, the family group.
Yes, it's true.
No.
You see what it is?
They knew that some people tried to escape from the German camps.
They knew there's lots of prisoners in the area.
They knew also that, that they've taken the Jewish, Jewish people for-- to the labor camp.
And looking at me, I never took a shave yet.
I was almost 17.
When you see a young boy, and-- you know, like a skeleton almost, they knew that I could not be a spy.
Those were experienced military men.
Yes.
It called Byelorussia, White Russia.
It-- the republic is today Byelorussia.
Yes.
Would be in the northern part of Byelorussia.
And the next city, which used to be the old Soviet Union, was Minsk.
Bialystok is more north yet.
Bialystok is more to Vilna or Vilnius.
It used to be Poland in '39.
And it's changed.
Baranowitsche.
No, no.
The trucks, I mean.
Food, what's that?
We didn't know what food means.
They treated us just like animals.
Even an animal, you get some water or, or hay or whatever.
We never got anything.
No, no.
We didn't come through Minsk.
We came directly from Bialystok to Baranowitsche.
No.
We didn't know anything about the Minsk ghetto.
We didn't know that there was concentration camps.
We didn't know there is gas chambers.
All that information, I received after the war.
The only thing I know is they're killing Jews.
They're killing prisoners.
That's all I know-- what we knew about it.
But anything else, we didn't know about it.
We were not aware of it.
No, it's unfortunate.
I tried to memorize.
And I can't.
There were-- but there were a lot-- the whole city, you go out maybe less than a kilometer, there's woods.
You go another half a mile, there's farms.
It's so close.
It's a wooded area that I really don't know.
There must be a name, but I don't remember, unfortunately.
It would be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] approximately southwest.
It used to be Poland.
Of Minsk, it would be a west or a little bit southwest.
Yeah.
Well, when I arrived over there, were mostly young men and young women.
I would say 75%, 80% were men.
And there were also young girls.
When we arrived, I was in heaven.
After, after almost two years, I got people, my own people.
I can talk to him.
We can lay down.
And we can, we can start living like a human being, even the conditions were very bad.
There was not sufficient food.
There were no, no showers or toilets.
That, that was a given that you go just in the woods.
That's OK.
Now, they started already-- they had already some food over there.
What they used to do before I arrived, they had some food.
Then I find out what they do.
At night, they used to have a group of men with guns already.
They had guns, but not enough.
And I tried to learn how to use a gun.
They showed us.
And from time to time, we used to go on patrol.
So we had to know how to use, how to use a gun.
And we used to go to the farms, you know, the farmers.
In that area, people use a lot of potatoes.
In the wintertime, they used to make big stacks of potatoes, put straw on top, and then dirt.
This protects it from freezing.
We knew about it.
So we had-- we had-- in the camp, we had some cows.
We had some horses and sled, sled.
And we used to go over there and actually steal it.
We open up one corner, take out as much potato we can, and cover it up.
They knew about it, the farmers.
But they had no control over it.
They were afraid.
They were afraid for the Germans.
They were afraid of the partisan.
Because if they do-- tell the local militia or the Germans-- there used to be always a combination between Germans and, and, and the local, local militia.
Used to be one or two Germans.
The rest of them were all local people.
They were serving with the German army.
They were afraid.
Because at night, another group would come and burn the house down.
The partisan would do it.
There were lots of partisans in the woods over there, many groups.
They were not yet under one command.
They were still not organized.
And-- and that's the way we started living a little bit.
And right away, I used to eat like a, like a horse.
Everybody get one bowl of soup.
I used to have three or four.
I was, I was so hungry from such a long time not eating.
And suddenly, I put on.
I became a regular man.
And then we started off to get organized.
And from little to little-- we used to shoot up.
Used to-- everything was done at night.
At day time, we were in the camp, the patrols outside see if no Germans come in.
In 1943 already, the partisan were very strong already.
The-- some of the, of the partisans had already-- there were regular Russian troops, which they did not surrender to the Germans.
They got contact with the, I assume, Russian Army.
And they used to, with airplanes, throw down guns and ammunition, paratroopers.
There were some paratroopers already over there.
And they start to organize the whole area.
Well, still, I mean, they stayed away from us.
Because they know it's a family camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we wanted to fight ourselves.
And I joined one, one of those fighting groups.
And we used to do explosive things with the trains that used to go over there.
Once, I was selected with four more men.
And we didn't know how to use dynamite.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were carrying the, the dynamite on our backs and find a spot in the wood.
We saw the, the railroad trucks go through.
But on both sides, the Germans cut the trees at least 100 feet or 150 feet.
And they had-- they had set up over there German and militia with machine guns, because they were so afraid of the partisan.
It was unbelievable.
Especially at night when you walk, you have five people walking.
And you've got the dry brush on the floor on the ground.
And it-- you walk, it sounds like 50 people walking.
